heb.paragraph: Chemosh (kemosh), Moab's national deity, "will go into exile, together with his priests and officials" (v.7). The exile of a nation's god was the ultimate defeat in ancient Near Eastern thought -- if the god goes into captivity, the nation is spiritually annihilated. Chemosh is mentioned on the Mesha Stele (c. 840 BC) and in the Torah's condemnation of Moabite religion (Num 21:29).

panels.ctx: The oracle against Moab is the longest of the oracles against the nations (47 verses), reflecting Moab's complex relationship with Israel -- kinship through Lot (Gen 19:37), periodic alliance, and recurring hostility. The oracle draws heavily on Isaiah 15-16, sometimes quoting it verbatim. The catalog of destroyed cities reads like a geographical roll call of annihilation: Nebo, Kiriathaim, Heshbon, Horonaim, Luhith, Arnon. The wine metaphor (vv.11-12) compares Moab to wine left undisturbed on its lees -- comfortable but about to be poured out and the jars smashed.

cross.note: Isaiah's parallel oracle against Moab -- Jeremiah draws extensively from it, creating an intertextual web.
"Woe to you, Moab! You are destroyed, people of Chemosh!" -- the Torah's original lament over Moab.
Ruth the Moabite -- the positive counterpoint to the general condemnation. Even from a condemned nation, faithful individuals emerge.

notes.note: The city-by-city destruction: Nebo captured, Kiriathaim disgraced, Heshbon plotting, Horonaim crying. The geographic sweep covers all of Moab from north to south.
"Since you trust in your deeds and riches" -- Moab's confidence in wealth and military strength is the root of its fall. Chemosh going into exile proves these trusts were misplaced.
The wine metaphor: Moab has been "at rest from youth, like wine left on its dregs." Undisturbed prosperity has produced complacency. Now the wine will be poured out and the jars smashed.
"How broken is the mighty scepter!" The fall of a powerful nation is itself a lament. Judgment does not eliminate grief -- even God mourns (v.31).
Calvin develops the wine metaphor: "Prolonged peace without repentance produces not maturity but stagnation. Moab's undisturbed prosperity was not blessing but judgment deferred."
The Moabite cities mentioned correspond to locations east of the Dead Sea in modern Jordan. Many are identifiable from the Mesha Stele and other ancient sources.
"Moab will be ashamed of Chemosh, as Israel was ashamed of Bethel" -- referring to the golden calf at Bethel that failed to protect the northern kingdom.

Lundbom notes the extensive borrowing from Isaiah 15-16 and suggests that Jeremiah "updates and extends" the earlier oracle for a new geopolitical context. The intertextuality creates a sense of prophetic tradition -- the same judgment declared across generations.

Brueggemann reads the wine metaphor as "a theology of disruption." Undisturbed comfort is not God's goal for any nation. The pouring-out is not vindictive but necessary -- stagnation must be broken for renewal.

heb.paragraph: "We have heard of Moab's pride (ga'on) -- how great is her arrogance! -- of her conceit, her pride and her insolence" (v.29). The fourfold repetition of pride-words piles accusation upon accusation. Moab's fundamental sin is the same as Babylon's: self-exaltation against the LORD. The nation "magnified herself against the LORD" (v.42), and therefore she will be destroyed.

panels.ctx: God himself laments over Moab: "I wail over Moab, for all Moab I cry out" (v.31). The divine grief within the judgment oracle echoes Jeremiah 12:7-13, where God mourned over Judah. Judgment is not cold indifference but grieved necessity. The chapter closes with a surprising promise: "Yet I will restore the fortunes of Moab in days to come" (v.47). Even the condemned nation has a future beyond judgment.

cross.note: Isaiah's parallel lament over Moab's pride -- the same vocabulary of arrogance and grief.
Edom's pride receives the same treatment: "The pride of your heart has deceived you." National arrogance is a universal prophetic target.

notes.note: The pride catalog: pride, arrogance, conceit, haughtiness, insolence. Five synonyms to ensure the point is unmistakable. Moab's boasting is "empty" (v.30).
God weeps over Moab: "I weep for you, as Jazer weeps." The vineyards of Sibmah will produce no more wine. The divine lament within the judgment oracle is striking.
"Moab will be destroyed as a nation because she defied the LORD." Pride against God is the capital offense among the nations.
"Yet I will restore the fortunes of Moab." The restoration promise -- unexpected and gracious -- opens a future beyond the destruction.
Calvin reads the restoration promise as evidence of "God's universal mercy. Even nations under the severest judgment retain a future in God's purpose. Destruction is never the last word."
These verses closely parallel Numbers 21:28-29 and 24:17, drawing on ancient poetry about Moab's original defeat. The prophetic oracle incorporates historical memory.

Lundbom notes that the restoration promise (v.47) mirrors similar promises for Ammon (49:6) and Elam (49:39). The oracles against the nations include seeds of hope -- judgment is penultimate, not ultimate.

Brueggemann reads God's tears over Moab as "the most unexpected feature of the oracle." The God who commands destruction also grieves it. This dual action -- judging and mourning simultaneously -- defines Jeremiah's theology.

rows.label: vv.11-17
rows.text: Wine on Its Lees: Complacency Shattered

lit.note: The longest oracle against the nations. The wine metaphor and divine weeping make this more than mere denunciation -- God grieves the judgment he pronounces.

themes.note: Judgment at 9 with the comprehensive destruction. Mercy at 4 reflects both God's tears and the closing restoration promise.

hebtext.note: Chemosh (kemosh), Moab's national deity, "will go into exile, together with his priests and officials" (v.7). The exile of a nation's god was the ultimate defeat in ancient Near Eastern thought -- if the god goes into captivity, the nation is spiritually annihilated. Chemosh is mentioned on the Mesha Stele (c. 840 BC) and in the Torah's condemnation of Moabite religion (Num 21:29).

"We have heard of Moab's pride (ga'on) -- how great is her arrogance! -- of her conceit, her pride and her insolence" (v.29). The fourfold repetition of pride-words piles accusation upon accusation. Moab's fundamental sin is the same as Babylon's: self-exaltation against the LORD. The nation "magnified herself against the LORD" (v.42), and therefore she will be destroyed.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Oracle Against Moab within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
